nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal zones including estuaries, seagrass beds, mangrove swamps, and reef flats; typically found at depths of 3–30 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on molluscs, crustaceans, worms, and small invertebrates. In captivity, tends to accept chopped shrimp, clams, mussels, squid, and krill. Hard-shelled prey such as clams and crabs are generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: A personable Indo-Pacific puffer with a milky white body and tan-brown markings; generally considered one of the milder puffer species but still semi-aggressive, and best kept singly in a fish-only system with large, robust tankmates.
careNotes:
Arothron immaculatus is distributed across the Indo-West Pacific from the Red Sea and East Africa through Indonesia and southern Japan. In the wild, it tends to inhabit shallow coastal zones including estuaries, mangrove swamps, and seagrass beds, and has been reported to tolerate reduced salinities as a juvenile — some sources market juveniles as 'brackish' specimens. Adults are more accurately described as marine fish and are generally kept at full marine salinity in aquariums.

The species has a distinctive beak-like structure formed by fused teeth that continues to grow throughout life; hard-shelled foods such as clams, mussels, and crabs are generally advised to prevent dental overgrowth. Body coloration is typically milky white with tan and brown overtones and yellow highlights on fins, mouth, and eyes; the species has been reported to alter its coloration based on mood or stress.

Arothron immaculatus is regarded by many hobbyists as one of the more personable and approachable puffer species, and individuals have been known to recognize their owners. Despite this relatively mild reputation among puffers, it will consume invertebrates and small fish and is not considered reef-safe.

Like all members of Tetraodontidae, this species can inflate its body by ingesting water when threatened. Allowing it to inhale air should be avoided as this can be harmful; transfers are generally recommended using a container rather than a net. The flesh contains tetrodotoxin and is highly toxic if consumed; the toxin is also present in the skin and organs.

Minimum tank size recommendations vary among sources: LiveAquaria recommends 120 US gallons (approximately 454 L), AquaticCommunity recommends 100 US gallons (approximately 379 L), and Aqua Imports recommends 90 US gallons (approximately 340 L). The averaged figure of approximately 390 L has been rounded up to 400 L to err on the side of caution given the species' active swimming habits and territorial tendencies. Heavy filtration and a protein skimmer are generally advised given the species' messy feeding habits.

This species is euryhaline and has occasionally been observed in brackish waters (salinity around 15 ppt), but full marine conditions (SG 1.020–1.025) are recommended for long-term captive care. Temperature recommendations differ slightly between FishBase (24.3–29.3°C, based on wild habitat data) and hobby sources (22.2–25.6°C); the values used here (23–27°C) represent an average across sources.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species-specific product page; provided adult size (12"), minimum tank size (120 gal), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.020–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), care level (easy), temperament (semi-aggressive), and reef safety (no). Original manifest URL pointed to the puffers collection page; navigated to species page via search result.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Arothron immaculatus, Bloch & Schneider 1801), taxonomy (Tetraodontidae / Tetraodontiformes), adult size (37.5 cm TL), native range (Red Sea and East Africa to Indonesia and Japan), temperature (24.3–29.3°C), depth range (3–30 m), and diet (trophic level 3.3, omnivorous benthic feeder). Temperature range is wider than hobby sources; values averaged across all sources.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12"/30 cm), minimum tank size (100 gal/400 L), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), salinity (1.020–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), care level (intermediate), temperament, diet, and reef safety (not reef safe). Minor disagreement on care level versus LiveAquaria (intermediate vs easy); intermediate adopted as the averaged/conservative value.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Confirmed species is euryhaline but primarily marine; noted juvenile brackish tolerance (down to ~15 ppt). Provided tank size recommendation of 90 gal (340 L), temperature 75–82°F, and diet notes. Contributed to tank-size averaging.
